6.1 Revision on Congruency of Triangles>ASA congruency
6.1 Revision on Congruency of Triangles>RHS Congruency

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What does the SSS congruence criterion state for two triangles?

options.A: Two sides and the included angle are equal.
options.B: All three corresponding angles are equal.
options.C: All three corresponding sides are equal in length.
options.D: The hypotenuse and one leg are equal.

questions.hint: Look at the letters: SSS means three pairs of sides.
questions.explanation: The SSS criterion requires all three corresponding sides to be congruent. Option C correctly states this, while option A describes SAS and option D describes RHS.

source_trace.content_anchor: Side- Side- Side congruency: When two triangles have equal corresponding sides, then the triangles are congruent.

explanations_tiered.definition: SSS congruence criterion states that if all three sides of one triangle are equal to all three sides of another triangle, the triangles are congruent.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Congruent triangles have identical size and shape. The SSS criterion provides a way to prove congruence using only side lengths, without measuring any angles.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of building two triangles with three sticks. If all three sticks match in length, the triangles will be exactly the same shape and size.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: SSS congruence means side-side-side correspondence. If \(AB = DE\), \(BC = EF\), and \(CA = FD\), then \(\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: SSS congruence is a sufficient condition. By the law of cosines, equal side lengths force equal corresponding angles, thus the triangles are congruent.
reasoning_steps: Identify all three side pairs in the two triangles.
Check if each side in one triangle equals the corresponding side in the other.
If all three pairs are equal, conclude the triangles are congruent by SSS.
misconceptions: Having two sides equal and one angle equal does not guarantee congruence unless the angle is between the sides (SAS).
Matching sides in the wrong order (e.g., \(AB = EF\) instead of \(AB = DE\)) leads to false conclusions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: SSS: all three sides equal \( \Rightarrow \) triangles congruent.

glossary_entry.definition: A criterion stating that two triangles are congruent if all three sides of one triangle are equal in length to the three sides of the other triangle.

flashcard.front: What does SSS stand for in triangle congruence?
flashcard.back: Side-Side-Side: all three corresponding sides are equal in length.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which congruence criterion uses only side lengths?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: SSS (Side-Side-Side). If all three sides are equal, the triangles are congruent.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two triangles are congruent if all three sides of one triangle equal the three sides of the other.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students try to prove with only two equal sides. Emphasize all three pairs are needed.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply postulates and theorems to prove congruency of triangles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. SSS requires all three corresponding side pairs to be equal.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which set of equal parts is sufficient to prove two triangles congruent by the SAS criterion?

options.A: Two angles and a non-included side.
options.B: Two sides and the included angle.
options.C: Two sides and a non-included angle.

questions.hint: The 'A' in SAS stands for the angle between the two sides.
questions.explanation: SAS congruence requires two sides and the included angle (the angle between those sides). Option B matches, while option A describes AAS and option C does not guarantee congruence.

source_trace.content_anchor: Side- Angle- Side congruency: When two triangles have two pair of congruent sides and one pair of congruent angles between the sides

explanations_tiered.definition: SAS congruence criterion states that if two sides and the included angle of one triangle equal two sides and the included angle of another triangle, the triangles are congruent.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The included angle is critical; it must be between the two known sides. Without that condition, congruence is not guaranteed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine two sticks forming a hinge. If the sticks match in length and the hinge angle matches, the triangles are the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If \(AB = DE\), \(AC = DF\), and \(\angle A = \angle D\), then \(\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF\) by SAS.
explanations_tiered.advanced: SAS follows from the law of cosines: given two sides and the included angle, the third side is uniquely determined, forcing congruence.
reasoning_steps: Identify two sides in each triangle that correspond.
Verify the angle between those sides in both triangles.
If both side pairs and the included angle match, apply SAS.
misconceptions: Believing any two sides and any angle suffice is wrong; the angle must lie between the two sides.
Confusing SAS with ASS (non-included angle) which does not guarantee congruence.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: SAS: side-angle-side, with angle between the sides.

glossary_entry.definition: A criterion stating two triangles are congruent if two sides and the included angle are respectively equal.

flashcard.front: What does SAS mean in triangle congruence?
flashcard.back: Side-Angle-Side: two sides and the angle between them are equal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which angle matters in the SAS criterion?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The included angle between the two known sides.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two triangles are congruent if two sides and the included angle of one equal the corresponding parts of the other.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: SAS: two sides + the angle between them.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Highlight the difference between SAS and ASS. Use diagrams to show why ASS fails.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply postulates and theorems to prove congruency of triangles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. SAS requires two sides and the angle between those sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which pair of congruent parts is necessary and sufficient for ASA congruence?

options.A: Two angles and the included side.
options.C: Two sides and the included angle.

questions.hint: The 'S' in ASA is the side between the two angles.
questions.explanation: ASA requires two angles and the side between them to be equal. Option A is correct, while option B is too vague and option C describes SAS.

source_trace.content_anchor: Angle- Side- Angle congruency: When two triangles have two pair of congruent angles and one pair of congruent sides between the angles

explanations_tiered.definition: ASA congruence criterion states that if two angles and the included side of one triangle equal two angles and the included side of another triangle, the triangles are congruent.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The side must be between the two angles. Once two angles are known, the third angle is fixed, but the side uniquely determines the size.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you know two corners of a triangle and the length of the edge between them, you can draw only one triangle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If \(\angle B = \angle E\), \(BC = EF\), and \(\angle C = \angle F\), then \(\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF\) by ASA.
explanations_tiered.advanced: ASA is derived from the fact that two angles determine the third via angle sum, and the included side fixes the scale uniquely in Euclidean geometry.
reasoning_steps: Locate two angles in each triangle that correspond.
Verify the side between those angles in both triangles.
If both angle pairs and the included side match, apply ASA.
misconceptions: Confusing ASA with AAS (non-included side) which is also valid but requires different matching.
Thinking any two angles are enough without checking which side is given.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: ASA: two angles + the side between them.

glossary_entry.definition: Two triangles are congruent if two angles and the included side of one equal the corresponding parts of the other.

flashcard.front: What does ASA stand for in triangle congruence?
flashcard.back: Angle-Side-Angle: two angles and the side between them are equal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which side is used in ASA?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The side between the two given angles (included side).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two triangles are congruent if two angles and the side between them match.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: ASA: two angles + the side between them.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that ASA and AAS are equivalent using triangle angle sum.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply postulates and theorems to prove congruency of triangles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. ASA requires two angles and the side between them.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The RHS congruence criterion applies only to which type of triangles?
questions.hint: RHS stands for Right angle, Hypotenuse, Side.
questions.explanation: RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) is a special congruence rule for right triangles only. Option C is correct; the other triangle types do not have a hypotenuse.

source_trace.content_anchor: Right- angle hypotenuse side congruency: When two right triangles have equal hypotenuse and equal one side (leg)

explanations_tiered.definition: RHS congruence criterion states that if the hypotenuse and one leg of one right triangle equal the hypotenuse and one leg of another right triangle, the triangles are congruent.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This is a special case of congruence for right triangles only, derived from the Pythagorean theorem and SSS.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In right triangles, matching the longest side (hypotenuse) and any one leg is enough to prove they are identical.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For right triangles \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle DEF\) with \(\angle C = \angle F = 90^\circ\), if hypotenuse \(AB = DE\) and leg \(BC = EF\), then \(\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF\) by RHS.
explanations_tiered.advanced: RHS follows from Pythagoras: given hypotenuse and one leg, the other leg is uniquely determined, then SSS applies.
reasoning_steps: Confirm both triangles are right-angled.
Check the hypotenuse (longest side) in each triangle.
Check one corresponding leg.
If both match, apply RHS to conclude congruence.
misconceptions: Applying RHS to non-right triangles — the hypotenuse exists only in right triangles.
Thinking any two sides work; the hypotenuse must be one of the matching sides.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: RHS: right triangle, hypotenuse + one leg equal.

glossary_entry.definition: A congruence criterion for right triangles: if the hypotenuse and one leg of one right triangle equal the hypotenuse and one leg of another, the triangles are congruent.

flashcard.front: What does RHS stand for in triangle congruence?
flashcard.back: Right angle, Hypotenuse, Side — applies only to right triangles.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which triangle type has a hypotenuse?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Only right triangles have a hypotenuse, used in RHS congruence.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two right triangles are congruent if their hypotenuses and one leg are equal.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: RHS: only for right triangles, hypotenuse + leg.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that RHS is a shortcut for SSS in right triangles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply postulates and theorems to prove congruency of triangles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. RHS applies only to right-angled triangles.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Two plane figures are defined as similar if which two conditions hold?
questions.hint: Remember: same shape, possibly different size.
questions.explanation: Similarity requires all corresponding angles congruent and corresponding sides proportional (same scale factor). Option B matches; equal sides would mean congruence, not similarity.

source_trace.content_anchor: Two plane figures are similar if their corresponding angles are congruent and the ratios of their corresponding sides are proportional. This common ratio is called the scale factor.

explanations_tiered.definition: Similar figures have the same shape but not necessarily the same size: all corresponding angles are equal and all corresponding sides are in the same ratio.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The scale factor is the constant ratio between corresponding side lengths. Congruent figures are a special case of similarity with scale factor 1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Similar figures are like enlargements or reductions — same angles, sides scaled by the same factor.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If \(\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF\), then \(\angle A = \angle D\), \(\angle B = \angle E\), \(\angle C = \angle F\), and \(\frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF} = \frac{CA}{FD} = k\) where \(k\) is the scale factor.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Similarity preserves angle measure and ratio of distances, forming an equivalence relation on the set of plane figures under scaling transformations.
reasoning_steps: Compare all corresponding angles. They must be equal.
Compare ratios of corresponding sides. All ratios must be the same value.
If both conditions hold, the figures are similar.
misconceptions: Thinking equal sides are needed — that is congruence, not similarity.
Believing proportional angles is a condition — angles are either equal or not; proportionality applies only to sides.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Similar: equal angles, proportional sides. Scale factor = side ratio.

glossary_entry.definition: Two plane figures are similar if corresponding angles are congruent and corresponding side lengths are proportional.

flashcard.front: What two conditions define similar figures?
flashcard.back: Corresponding angles equal and corresponding sides proportional.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the difference between congruent and similar?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Congruent: same size and shape. Similar: same shape, sizes may differ (sides proportional).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two figures are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and their corresponding sides are proportional.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use maps and scale drawings to illustrate similarity in real life.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between congruent and similar plane figures
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Similar requires equal angles and proportional sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF\) with \(AB = 4\), \(BC = 5\), \(CA = 2\) and \(DE = 8\), \(EF = 10\), \(FD = 4\). What is the scale factor from \(\triangle ABC\) to \(\triangle DEF\)?
questions.hint: Scale factor = \( \frac{\text{side in DEF}}{\text{corresponding side in ABC}} \).
questions.explanation: Scale factor = \(\frac{DE}{AB} = \frac{8}{4} = 2\). Option B is correct. Options A and C use wrong ratios; D is an individual side length, not a ratio.

source_trace.content_anchor: DE/AB = 8/4 = 2, EF/BC = 10/5 = 2, FD/CA = 4/2 = 2, thus common ratio = 2

explanations_tiered.definition: Scale factor is the constant ratio of corresponding side lengths in similar figures.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Any ratio of a side in the image to the corresponding side in the original gives the scale factor. It must be the same for all side pairs.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a triangle is enlarged, each side multiplies by the same number. That number is the scale factor.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Scale factor \(k = \frac{DE}{AB} = \frac{8}{4} = 2\). This means every side in \(\triangle DEF\) is twice the length of the corresponding side in \(\triangle ABC\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scale factor applies to all linear dimensions. Here \(k = 2\). Note that areas scale by \(k^2 = 4\), but perimeters scale by \(k = 2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify corresponding sides. \(AB\) corresponds to \(DE\).
Step 2: Write ratio of larger to smaller: \(\frac{DE}{AB} = \frac{8}{4}\).
Step 3: Simplify: \(\frac{8}{4} = 2\). The scale factor is 2.
reasoning_steps: Pick one pair of corresponding sides.
Divide the image side length by the original side length.
The result is the scale factor.
misconceptions: Using the reciprocal (0.5 instead of 2) if the order is reversed without specifying which to which.
Adding side lengths instead of dividing.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scale factor = image side / original side. Always >1 for enlargement.

glossary_entry.definition: The constant ratio between corresponding side lengths of similar figures.

flashcard.front: How is the scale factor calculated from similar triangles?
flashcard.back: Divide a side length in the second triangle by the corresponding side in the first: \(k = \frac{DE}{AB}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate scale factor: ABC sides 4,5,2; DEF sides 8,10,4.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The factor by which all side lengths of a figure are multiplied to obtain a similar figure.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce checking multiple side pairs to confirm similarity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State and use the criteria for similarity of triangles

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Quadrilaterals MNPQ and RSTU are similar. If \(MN = 18\), \(RS = 30\), and \(PQ = 15\), what is the length of \(TU\)?
questions.explanation: The scale factor is \(\frac{RS}{MN} = \frac{30}{18} = \frac{5}{3}\). Then \(TU = PQ \times \frac{5}{3} = 15 \times \frac{5}{3} = 25\). Option C is correct; A and B use wrong ratios.

source_trace.content_anchor: MN PQ RS TU 18 15 30 y y=25

explanations_tiered.definition: Corresponding sides of similar figures are proportional, allowing unknown lengths to be found via cross-multiplication.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Set up a proportion between known corresponding sides, then solve for the unknown.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two shapes are similar, side lengths follow the same multiplication rule. Find the missing one by matching the right pairs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The constant of proportionality \(k = \frac{30}{18} = \frac{5}{3}\) applies to all linear dimensions. Thus \(TU = k \cdot PQ = 25\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify corresponding sides: MN corresponds to RS; PQ corresponds to TU.
reasoning_steps: Match corresponding sides in the similarity statement.
Set up a proportion using the known and unknown sides.
Solve for the unknown by cross-multiplication.
misconceptions: Matching non-corresponding sides (e.g., MN to TU) leading to wrong proportion.
Flipping ratios incorrectly (using TU/15 = 30/18 instead of 18/30 = 15/TU).

glossary_entry.definition: When figures are similar, ratios of corresponding side lengths are equal.

flashcard.front: How do you find a missing side in similar figures?
flashcard.back: Set up a proportion using known corresponding sides and solve.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Similar quads: MN=18, RS=30, PQ=15, find TU.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An equation stating that two ratios are equal, used to find unknown sides in similar figures.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize correctly identifying corresponding vertices from the similarity statement.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems on perimeters and areas of similar triangles and similar polygons

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another triangle, why is the third pair automatically congruent?

options.C: By the angle sum property of triangles

questions.hint: All triangles have a total angle sum of \(180^\circ\).
questions.explanation: The angle sum property states that the sum of interior angles in any triangle is \(180^\circ\). Thus, if two pairs match, the third must also match. Option C is correct; the other theorems do not guarantee equal angles.

source_trace.content_anchor: the third pair will then automatically be equal by the angle sum property of triangles

explanations_tiered.definition: AA similarity states that if two angles of one triangle equal two angles of another, the triangles are similar.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The angle sum property \(A+B+C=180^\circ\) forces the third angles to be equal automatically, making it AAA effectively.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two corners match, the third must match because all triangles have \(180^\circ\). Like a sum puzzle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given \(\angle A = \angle D\) and \(\angle B = \angle E\), then \(\angle C = 180^\circ - \angle A - \angle B = 180^\circ - \angle D - \angle E = \angle F\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The angle sum property is a theorem of Euclidean geometry independent of side lengths, ensuring two-angle equality suffices for similarity.
reasoning_steps: Note the sum of angles in any triangle is \(180^\circ\).
Compute third angle in first triangle: \(180^\circ - \text{(sum of two given angles)}\).
Same result in second triangle, so third angles are equal.
misconceptions: Thinking two equal angles are not enough — but the angle sum automatically gives the third.
Confusing similarity with congruence — equal angles alone give similarity, not congruence.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Two equal angles ⇒ third equal automatically (sum 180°).

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of the interior angles of a triangle is always \(180^\circ\).

flashcard.front: Why is AA enough for similarity instead of AAA?
flashcard.back: The third angle is forced equal by the \(180^\circ\) angle sum property.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What theorem guarantees that two equal angles give a third equal angle?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The sum of interior angles in a triangle is \(180^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with numeric examples: 50° and 60° give third 70° automatically.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State and use the criteria for similarity of triangles viz. AAA, SSS and SAS
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Angle sum property forces the third angle to match.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If the corresponding sides of two triangles are in the ratio \(3:5\), what can be concluded about the triangles?
questions.hint: SSS similarity requires proportional sides, not necessarily equal.
questions.explanation: When all three side ratios are equal (here 3:5), the SSS similarity theorem applies, so the triangles are similar. Option B is correct; congruence would require ratio 1:1.

source_trace.content_anchor: If the corresponding three sides of two triangles are proportional to each other, then the triangles are similar.

explanations_tiered.definition: SSS similarity theorem: If the three sides of one triangle are proportional to the three sides of another, the triangles are similar.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Proportional sides mean the scale factor is constant, forcing corresponding angles to be equal via the law of cosines.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If sides of one triangle are all 3/5 of the other's sides, the triangles have the same shape — they are similar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle DEF\), if \(\frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF} = \frac{AC}{DF} = k\), then by SSS similarity, \(\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The constant ratio \(k\) is the scale factor. SSS similarity is the contrapositive of the SSS congruence criterion extended to proportional lengths.
reasoning_steps: Find the ratio of each pair of corresponding sides.
Check if all three ratios are equal.
If yes, conclude similarity by SSS similarity theorem.
misconceptions: Thinking proportional sides imply congruence — they imply similarity, congruence only when ratio = 1.
Assuming two proportional side pairs are enough; all three pairs are required for SSS similarity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: SSS similarity: all three side ratios equal → similar triangles.

glossary_entry.definition: Two triangles are similar if all three pairs of corresponding sides are proportional.

flashcard.front: What condition proves similarity without any angle information?
flashcard.back: SSS similarity: all three corresponding side pairs are proportional.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When are two triangles similar by SSS?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: When all three corresponding side pairs are in the same proportion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A theorem: if corresponding sides are proportional, the triangles are similar.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: SSS similarity: equal side ratios, not equal lengths.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State and use the criteria for similarity of triangles viz. AAA, SSS and SAS
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. SSS similarity applies when all three side ratios are equal.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Two triangles have two pairs of proportional sides and the included angles equal. Which similarity theorem applies?
questions.hint: The name matches the given conditions: Side-Angle-Side.
questions.explanation: SAS similarity requires two proportional side pairs with the included angle congruent. Option C is correct; AA needs angles, SSS needs three side ratios, and RHS is for right triangles only.

source_trace.content_anchor: If the corresponding two sides of two triangles are proportional and the included angles are congruent, then the two triangles are similar.

explanations_tiered.definition: SAS similarity theorem: If two sides are proportional and the included angle is congruent, the triangles are similar.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The included angle must be between the two proportional sides. SAS similarity is analogous to SAS congruence but with a ratio instead of equality.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two sides are scaled by the same factor and the angle between them matches, the triangles are the same shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given \(\frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{AC}{DF}\) and \(\angle A = \angle D\), then \(\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF\) by SAS similarity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: SAS similarity can be derived from the law of cosines: proportional sides and equal included angle force the third side to be in the same proportion, making all three side ratios equal.
reasoning_steps: Identify the two side pairs that are proportional.
Verify the angle between those sides is congruent in both triangles.
If yes, apply SAS similarity theorem.
misconceptions: Applying SAS similarity with a non-included angle — the angle must be between the two sides.
Thinking SAS similarity requires the sides to be equal, not just proportional (that would be SAS congruence).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: SAS similarity: two proportional sides + included angle equal.

glossary_entry.definition: Two triangles are similar if two sides are proportional and the included angle is congruent.

flashcard.back: Two proportional side pairs and the included angle congruent.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Name the similarity theorem for two proportional sides and the included angle.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: If two sides are proportional and the included angle equal, the triangles are similar.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: SAS similarity: two sides proportional + included angle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize the word 'included' — critical for both SAS congruence and similarity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State and use the criteria for similarity of triangles viz. AAA, SSS and SAS
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. SAS similarity requires proportional sides and included angle.
